headline: Lewis and Clark Celebrate Christmas at Fort Mandan, 1804
body:
The biting wind, a sculptor of the northern plains, had long since begun its work, carving drifts and frosting the very breath of man and beast. It was December 25, 1804, and the Corps of Discovery, those intrepid souls charting the vast, untamed heart of a continent, found themselves ensconced within the sturdy palisades of Fort Mandan. Here, amidst the stark beauty of a North Dakota winter, a Christmas was to be observed, a brief, flickering ember of civilization in the immense wilderness. The day dawned not with the gentle carols of home, but with a robust, almost defiant, salute to the season. As Joseph Whitehouse, one of the expedition's privates, meticulously recorded, “we ushred in the morning with a discharge of the Swivvel, and one round of Small arms of all the party. then another from the Swivel. then Capt. Clark presented a glass of brandy to each man of the party. we hoisted the american flag and each man had another Glass of brandy.” A fitting reveille for men who had traded hearth and home for the boundless unknown, their spirits buoyed by the potent warmth of brandy and the sight of their nation’s banner snapping against the frigid sky.

The air, crisp and sharp, carried the scent of woodsmoke and anticipation. The thermometer, a marvel of scientific precision in such a remote locale, registered a mere 15 degrees at sunrise, climbing reluctantly to 20 by late afternoon, a testament to the season's unyielding grip. Snow, though
